254	```
255	
256	---
257	
258	## Source priority by entity type
259	
260	### VC Fund — sources ranked by reliability
261	
262	| Priority | Source | Best for |
263	|----------|--------|----------|
264	| 1 | Fund's own website (About / Strategy / Team pages) | Thesis, partners, portfolio |
265	| 2 | Crunchbase organization profile | Rounds, AUM, portfolio, exits |
266	| 3 | SEC Form ADV (US funds) / regulatory filings | AUM, legal entity, GP |
267	| 4 | LinkedIn company page | Team, location, description |
268	| 5 | Press: fund announcement / close articles | AUM, thesis, LP details |
269	| 6 | Dealroom / PitchBook | Portfolio, co-investors |
270	| 7 | startup.gov.vn / Viet.io (Vietnam) | Local funds, ecosystem role |
271	
272	### Angel Investor — sources ranked by reliability
273	
274	| Priority | Source | Best for |
275	|----------|--------|----------|
276	| 1 | LinkedIn profile | Experience, education, current role |
277	| 2 | Crunchbase person record | Investment history, board seats |
278	| 3 | AngelList / WellFound profile | Ticket size, sector, stage preference |
279	| 4 | Personal website / blog | Investment thesis, portfolio |
280	| 5 | Press: "angel investor X backed Y" | Specific investments |
281	| 6 | Twitter/X bio | Current focus, interests |
282	
283	**PII wall:** Stop at professional data. Do not search for or attempt to extract:
284	- DOB, age
285	- ID numbers, passport numbers
286	- Tax IDs, social security numbers
287	- Home addresses
288	- Personal phone numbers
289	- KYC status, accredited investor verification
290	
291	If a source accidentally surfaces this data, **do not include it in the output**. Mark it in `pii_blocked`.
292	
293	### Startup — sources ranked by reliability
294	
295	| Priority | Source | Best for |
296	|----------|--------|----------|
297	| 1 | Company website (About, Product, Team pages) | Product, team, description |
298	| 2 | Crunchbase organization profile | Funding, investors, founders |
299	| 3 | Press: funding announcement | Round details, valuation |
300	| 4 | LinkedIn company page | Team size, location, industry |
301	| 5 | startup.gov.vn / Viet.io (Vietnam) | Local startups, ecosystem |
302	| 6 | FundedAPI | Real-time funding events |
303	| 7 | Founder LinkedIn profiles | Founder background |
304	| 8 | Pitch deck (if public on website) | Traction, USP, financials |
305	
306	---
307	
308	## Vietnam-specific sources
309	
310	Always include these when enriching Vietnam-based entities:
311	
312	| Source | URL | VC | Angel | Startup |
313	|--------|-----|-----|-------|---------|
314	| startup.gov.vn | http://startup.gov.vn/ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
315	| Viet.io | https://viet.io/ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
316	| BSSC Portal | https://congthongtin.bssc.vn/ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
317	| VnExpress (tech) | https://vnexpress.net/ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
318	| DealStreetAsia | https://www.dealstreetasia.com/ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
319	| Vietnam Investment Review | https://vir.com.vn/ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
320	| NIC Scale X / VietLeap pages | https://vnic.nic.gov.vn/ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
321	
322	---
323	
324	## Batch enrichment mode
325	
326	When enriching multiple entities (e.g., all 25 NIC-supported startups):
327	
328	1. **Parallel search phase:** Run `web_search` queries for 3-5 entities simultaneously (interleave queries so each entity gets 1-2 queries per batch)
329	2. **Parallel fetch phase:** Gather all high-signal URLs across entities, fetch 4-6 at a time with `fetch_content` `urls`
330	3. **Sequential output:** Write each profile to `outputs/enrichment/{type}_{slug}.json` as it completes
331	4. **Summary index:** Create `outputs/enrichment/_index.json` listing all enriched entities with collection stats
332	
333	## Important rules
334	
335	1. **Always record provenance.** Every field must have a `source_url`. If you can't find a source, mark it `unavailable` — don't invent data.
336	2. **Never collect PII for individuals.** The `pii_blocked` list in the angel schema is hard enforcement, not a guideline.
337	3. **Prefer entity-owned sources.** The startup's own website beats Crunchbase beats a press article.
338	4. **Flag low confidence.** If you're inferring from context (e.g., guessing sector from company description), mark it `low`.
339	5. **Date everything.** Include `enriched_at` timestamp so downstream consumers know data freshness.
340	6. **Do not paywall-shim.** If data is behind a Crunchbase/PitchBook paywall, mark it `unavailable` and note the paywall. Do not try to bypass.
341	7. **Vietnam first.** For Vietnamese entities, always check startup.gov.vn, Viet.io, and VnExpress before global sources.
342

file.content:
---
name: entity-enrichment
description: Collect structured data about VCs, angel investors, and startups from multiple public sources. Use when the user needs to enrich entity profiles, build a deal-flow database, or populate a matching framework from web research. Skips PII for individuals — only public professional data.
allowed-tools: Bash, web_search, fetch_content, get_search_content, document_parse, code_search, write, read, edit
---

# Entity Enrichment

Collect structured profiles for VC funds, angel investors, and startups by searching and extracting from multiple public sources in parallel. Outputs a provenance-tracked JSON profile with confidence flags per field.

## When to use

- Populating a deal-flow database with real company/investor data
- Enriching startup profiles before running a matching engine
- Building a seed dataset for a matchmaking platform
- Any time you need to collect structured entity data from the open web

## Entity types and field coverage

This skill targets three entity types with different collection strategies:

| Entity | Public sources | PII rule |
|--------|---------------|----------|
| **VC Fund** | Crunchbase, fund website, LinkedIn, SEC filings, press, startup.gov.vn, Viet.io, Dealroom, PitchBook | All public — funds are legal entities |
| **Angel Investor** | LinkedIn, Crunchbase person records, AngelList, Twitter/X, personal website, press mentions | **STRICT: no DOB, ID, passport, tax ID, KYC, accredited status, personal phone.** Only professional/public data. |
| **Startup** | Crunchbase, company website, LinkedIn, startup.gov.vn, Viet.io, BSSC Portal, FundedAPI, pitch deck (if public), press | All public — companies are legal entities |

## Collection workflow

### Phase 1: Source discovery (parallel search)

For each entity, run 4-6 searches with different angles simultaneously using `web_search` `queries` parameter:

**For a VC fund named "Example Ventures":**
```
1. "Example Ventures fund size AUM partners portfolio"
2. "Example Ventures investment thesis stage check size sectors"
3. "Example Ventures Crunchbase PitchBook profile"
4. "Example Ventures portfolio companies exits"
5. "Example Ventures Vietnam investments Asia Southeast Asia"
6. site:exampleventures.com "team" OR "portfolio" OR "strategy"
```

**For an angel investor named "Jane Nguyen":**
```
1. "Jane Nguyen angel investor portfolio investments"
2. "Jane Nguyen LinkedIn Crunchbase"
3. "Jane Nguyen startup investments exits"
4. "Jane Nguyen" site:linkedin.com
```
**NEVER search for:** DOB, passport, ID, tax ID, KYC, personal address, personal phone.
Only search for: name + "investor" / "angel" / "portfolio" / "startup" / "LinkedIn" / "investments".

**For a startup named "Example AI":**
```
1. "Example AI startup Crunchbase funding valuation"
2. "Example AI founder team LinkedIn"
3. "Example AI product customers revenue growth"
4. "Example AI competitors market"
5. "Example AI funding round investors"
6. site:example.ai
```

### Phase 2: Deep extraction (parallel fetch)

From Phase 1 results, identify the 3-6 highest-signal URLs and fetch them in parallel with `fetch_content` `urls` parameter:

**Priority URL types (ordered by signal strength):**
1. Crunchbase / PitchBook / Dealroom profile pages
2. The entity's own website (About, Team, Portfolio pages)
3. LinkedIn company/person page
4. Press releases announcing funding rounds
5. SEC Form D / ADV filings (US funds)
6. TechCrunch / VnExpress / DealStreetAsia coverage
7. startup.gov.vn / Viet.io listings (Vietnam-specific)

### Phase 3: Document extraction (if applicable)

If the startup has a public pitch deck or the VC has a public fund factsheet PDF:
- Use `document_parse` to extract text from the PDF
- Use LLM extraction prompt (see Section 5 of dealflow research doc) to pull structured fields

### Phase 4: Merge and annotate

Consolidate all extracted data into the output schema. For every field, record:

```json
{
  "field": "ticket_size",
  "value": "$500K-$2M",
  "source_url": "https://crunchbase.com/organization/example-ventures",
  "source_type": "crunchbase",
  "confidence": "high"
}
```

**Confidence rules:**
- `high` — from entity's own website, official filing, or Crunchbase verified profile
- `medium` — from press article, LinkedIn, or third-party database
- `low` — inferred from context, single mention, or outdated source
- `unavailable` — searched but not found in any source

### Phase 5: Output

Write the enriched profile to `outputs/enrichment/{entity_type}_{entity_slug}.json`.

---

## Output schema

### VC Fund

```json
{
  "entity_type": "vc",
  "enriched_at": "2026-07-18T...",
  "legal_information": {
    "fund_name": {"value": "...", "source_url": "...", "confidence": "high"},
    "legal_entity": {"value": "...", "source_url": "...", "confidence": "medium"},
    "country": {"value": "...", "source_url": "...", "confidence": "high"},
    "founded": {"value": 2020, "source_url": "...", "confidence": "high"},
    "aum": {"value": "...", "source_url": "...", "confidence": "medium"},
    "gp_partners": {"value": ["..."], "source_url": "...", "confidence": "high"},
    "website": {"value": "...", "source_url": "...", "confidence": "high"},
    "linkedin": {"value": "...", "source_url": "...", "confidence": "high"}
  },
  "investment_thesis": {
    "stage": {"value": ["seed", "series_a"], "source_url": "...", "confidence": "high"},
    "check_size": {"value": "$500K-$2M", "source_url": "...", "confidence": "high"},
    "sector_focus": {"value": ["ai", "fintech"], "source_url": "...", "confidence": "high"},
    "geography": {"value": ["Vietnam", "Southeast Asia"], "source_url": "...", "confidence": "high"}
  },
  "portfolio": {
    "companies": {"value": [{"name": "...", "round": "seed"}], "source_url": "...", "confidence": "medium"},
    "vietnam_exposure": {"value": true, "source_url": "...", "confidence": "medium"},
    "notable_exits": {"value": ["..."], "source_url": "...", "confidence": "medium"},
    "lead_co_invest": {"value": "both", "source_url": "...", "confidence": "low"},
    "follow_on_rate": {"value": null, "source_url": null, "confidence": "unavailable"}
  },
  "deal_sourcing": {
    "key_contacts": {"value": [{"name": "...", "role": "Partner", "linkedin": "..."}], "source_url": "...", "confidence": "medium"},
    "emails": {"value": ["..."], "source_url": "...", "confidence": "low"},
    "warm_intro_status": {"value": null, "source_url": null, "confidence": "unavailable"}
  },
  "collection_summary": {
    "total_fields": 20,
    "fields_collected": 17,
    "fields_unavailable": 3,
    "sources_used": ["crunchbase.com", "exampleventures.com", "techcrunch.com"],
    "gaps": ["follow_on_rate", "warm_intro_status", "lead_co_invest"]
  }
}
```

### Angel Investor

```json
{
  "entity_type": "angel",
  "enriched_at": "2026-07-18T...",
  "identity": {
    "full_name": {"value": "...", "source_url": "...", "confidence": "high"},
    "nationality": {"value": "...", "source_url": "...", "confidence": "medium"}
  },
  "investment_profile": {
    "ticket_size": {"value": "$25K-$100K", "source_url": "...", "confidence": "medium"},
    "stage": {"value": ["pre_seed", "seed"], "source_url": "...", "confidence": "medium"},
    "sector": {"value": ["ai", "healthtech"], "source_url": "...", "confidence": "medium"},
    "geography": {"value": ["Vietnam"], "source_url": "...", "confidence": "medium"},
    "frequency": {"value": null, "source_url": null, "confidence": "unavailable"},
    "lead_co_invest": {"value": null, "source_url": null, "confidence": "unavailable"}
  },
  "portfolio": {
    "investments": {"value": [{"name": "Startup X", "year": 2024}], "source_url": "...", "confidence": "medium"},
    "exits": {"value": [], "source_url": "...", "confidence": "medium"},
    "board_seats": {"value": [], "source_url": "...", "confidence": "low"},
    "notable_startups": {"value": ["Startup X"], "source_url": "...", "confidence": "medium"}
  },
  "professional": {
    "current_company": {"value": "...", "source_url": "...", "confidence": "high"},
    "role": {"value": "...", "source_url": "...", "confidence": "high"},
    "experience": {"value": ["..."], "source_url": "...", "confidence": "high"},
    "education": {"value": ["..."], "source_url": "...", "confidence": "high"},
    "linkedin": {"value": "...", "source_url": "...", "confidence": "high"}
  },
  "pii_blocked": ["dob", "id_passport", "tax_id", "kyc_status", "accredited_status", "personal_phone", "personal_address"],
  "collection_summary": {
    "total_fields": 18,
    "fields_collected": 13,
    "pii_skipped": 7,
    "fields_unavailable": 2,
    "sources_used": ["linkedin.com", "crunchbase.com", "angellist.com"],
    "gaps": ["frequency", "lead_co_invest"]
  }
}
```

### Startup

```json
{
  "entity_type": "startup",
  "enriched_at": "2026-07-18T...",
  "company": {
    "legal_entity": {"value": "...", "source_url": "...", "confidence": "high"},

---

## Source priority by entity type

### VC Fund — sources ranked by reliability

| Priority | Source | Best for |
|----------|--------|----------|
| 1 | Fund's own website (About / Strategy / Team pages) | Thesis, partners, portfolio |
| 2 | Crunchbase organization profile | Rounds, AUM, portfolio, exits |
| 3 | SEC Form ADV (US funds) / regulatory filings | AUM, legal entity, GP |
| 4 | LinkedIn company page | Team, location, description |
| 5 | Press: fund announcement / close articles | AUM, thesis, LP details |
| 6 | Dealroom / PitchBook | Portfolio, co-investors |
| 7 | startup.gov.vn / Viet.io (Vietnam) | Local funds, ecosystem role |

### Angel Investor — sources ranked by reliability

| Priority | Source | Best for |
|----------|--------|----------|
| 1 | LinkedIn profile | Experience, education, current role |
| 2 | Crunchbase person record | Investment history, board seats |
| 3 | AngelList / WellFound profile | Ticket size, sector, stage preference |
| 4 | Personal website / blog | Investment thesis, portfolio |
| 5 | Press: "angel investor X backed Y" | Specific investments |
| 6 | Twitter/X bio | Current focus, interests |

**PII wall:** Stop at professional data. Do not search for or attempt to extract:
- DOB, age
- ID numbers, passport numbers
- Tax IDs, social security numbers
- Home addresses
- Personal phone numbers
- KYC status, accredited investor verification

If a source accidentally surfaces this data, **do not include it in the output**. Mark it in `pii_blocked`.

### Startup — sources ranked by reliability

| Priority | Source | Best for |
|----------|--------|----------|
| 1 | Company website (About, Product, Team pages) | Product, team, description |
| 2 | Crunchbase organization profile | Funding, investors, founders |
| 3 | Press: funding announcement | Round details, valuation |
| 4 | LinkedIn company page | Team size, location, industry |
| 5 | startup.gov.vn / Viet.io (Vietnam) | Local startups, ecosystem |
| 6 | FundedAPI | Real-time funding events |
| 7 | Founder LinkedIn profiles | Founder background |
| 8 | Pitch deck (if public on website) | Traction, USP, financials |

---

## Vietnam-specific sources

Always include these when enriching Vietnam-based entities:

| Source | URL | VC | Angel | Startup |
|--------|-----|-----|-------|---------|
| startup.gov.vn | http://startup.gov.vn/ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Viet.io | https://viet.io/ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
| BSSC Portal | https://congthongtin.bssc.vn/ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
| VnExpress (tech) | https://vnexpress.net/ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| DealStreetAsia | https://www.dealstreetasia.com/ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Vietnam Investment Review | https://vir.com.vn/ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
| NIC Scale X / VietLeap pages | https://vnic.nic.gov.vn/ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |

---

## Batch enrichment mode

When enriching multiple entities (e.g., all 25 NIC-supported startups):

1. **Parallel search phase:** Run `web_search` queries for 3-5 entities simultaneously (interleave queries so each entity gets 1-2 queries per batch)
2. **Parallel fetch phase:** Gather all high-signal URLs across entities, fetch 4-6 at a time with `fetch_content` `urls`
3. **Sequential output:** Write each profile to `outputs/enrichment/{type}_{slug}.json` as it completes
4. **Summary index:** Create `outputs/enrichment/_index.json` listing all enriched entities with collection stats

## Important rules

1. **Always record provenance.** Every field must have a `source_url`. If you can't find a source, mark it `unavailable` — don't invent data.
2. **Never collect PII for individuals.** The `pii_blocked` list in the angel schema is hard enforcement, not a guideline.
3. **Prefer entity-owned sources.** The startup's own website beats Crunchbase beats a press article.
4. **Flag low confidence.** If you're inferring from context (e.g., guessing sector from company description), mark it `low`.
5. **Date everything.** Include `enriched_at` timestamp so downstream consumers know data freshness.
6. **Do not paywall-shim.** If data is behind a Crunchbase/PitchBook paywall, mark it `unavailable` and note the paywall. Do not try to bypass.
7. **Vietnam first.** For Vietnamese entities, always check startup.gov.vn, Viet.io, and VnExpress before global sources.

content.text:
I've now got the full picture. Let me give you a verification verdict before I ask the forks that shape the design.

## What I found (grounding)

- **`pi` RPC is real and usable.** `pi --mode rpc` speaks line-delimited JSON over stdio: request `{"id", "type":"prompt", ...}` → response `{"id","type":"response","success",...}`, streaming. So a long-lived pi process you feed tasks to and read results from is a confirmed primitive — this is the backbone your "isolated runtime + RPC" idea needs.
- **`pi` is the programmatic runtime; `feynman` is the research shell on top of it.** feynman adds web tools (`web_search`, `fetch_content`, `document_parse`, `summarize`, `deepresearch`, `watch`) and a research prompt. Your crawler/enrichment agents want feynman's tools; pi gives the clean RPC/JSON/`-p` surface, tool allow-lists (`--tools`), skill loading (`--skill`), and per-session isolation (`--session-id`, `--no-session`).
- **You already have 3 domain skills for this exact problem**, written as pi/feynman skills: `entity-enrichment` (web → profiles), `profile-extraction` (deck/site → JSON), `dealflow-match` (rule-filter → SBERT → LLM-explain + email drafts). The "agents" in your platform are essentially *these skills, each loaded into an isolated pi/feynman process*.
- **Current settings:** feynman default model `deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro`, 7 authenticated models across 2 providers, web access via `pi-web-access`. Usable as-is.

## My honest read on your idea

**Feasible, and the pieces fit — but "a data platform with *only* agents" needs one correction.** A data platform still needs a non-agent *spine*: durable storage, a schema/contract, a supervisor that spawns/monitors runtimes, and the RPC transport itself. Agents belong at the **fuzzy edges** (crawl, extract, enrich, match-explain, draft) — tasks that are genuinely LLM-shaped. Using LLM agents for the deterministic plumbing (dedup, upserts, vector indexing, scheduling math) is slow, costly, and non-reproducible. The winning shape is **agents at the edges, deterministic code at the spine** — not agents-all-the-way-down. I'll push on this in the questions.

Before I propose approaches, four forks genuinely change the architecture:

questions.question: What's the target for this build? It sets how much spine-infrastructure vs. demo-wow we invest in.

options.label: 48h hackathon demo
options.description: VAIC 2026 deliverable. Prioritize an end-to-end visible flow (profile → match → email → meeting) with a thin but real agent+RPC backbone. Cut anything not on the demo path.

options.label: Serious foundation, demo first
options.description: Build the agent-data-platform properly (durable storage, supervisor, contracts) but sequence so a demo is possible early. More upfront infra.

options.label: Architecture spike only
options.description: Prove the agent-runtime-as-worker + RPC pattern works with 1-2 real agents; defer the full matchmaker product.

questions.question: How literally do you want 'only agents'? This is the core architecture decision.

options.label: Agents at edges, code spine (Recommended)
options.description: pi/feynman agents do crawl/extract/enrich/match-explain/draft. Deterministic Python owns storage, dedup, vector index, scheduling, and the supervisor. Reproducible + cheap.

options.label: Agents for all logic, thin code shell
options.description: Push even orchestration/routing decisions into agents; code is only storage + transport. More 'agentic' but costlier and less deterministic.

options.label: Truly agent-only
options.description: Every operation including plumbing is an agent action. I'd advise against this — expensive, flaky, hard to demo. Happy to explain why.

questions.question: How should the isolated runtimes communicate?

options.label: Star: supervisor ↔ each agent (Recommended)
options.description: One Python supervisor holds an RPC stdio channel to each pi/feynman process. Agents don't talk directly; supervisor routes work + collects results. Simplest, matches pi's stdio model.

options.label: Mesh: agent ↔ agent RPC
options.description: Agents call each other directly over a shared bus/socket. More 'distributed' but needs a broker, addressing, and failure handling you'd build yourself.

options.label: Hybrid
options.description: Star for control, plus a shared artifact/event store agents read/write so they coordinate indirectly (blackboard pattern).

questions.question: Which runtime should the agents run on?

options.label: feynman for crawlers, pi for the rest (Recommended)
options.description: Crawl/enrich/extract agents run on feynman (web tools). Match/draft/schedule agents run on pi (RPC, skills, cheaper). Best tool per job.

options.label: All feynman
options.description: Every agent is a feynman process. Uniform, web tools everywhere, but heavier and less clean RPC surface for non-research tasks.

options.label: All pi
options.description: Every agent is raw pi with skills + selected tools. Cleanest RPC/isolation; you'd wire web-fetch tools in explicitly where crawlers need them.

content.text:
Locked in: **serious foundation (demo-first) · agents at edges, code spine · star RPC · feynman-crawlers/pi-rest**. That resolves the biggest forks. Now the shape of the platform itself — three ways to organize it:

## Approach A — Agent Fleet + Blackboard Spine  *(recommended)*

Deterministic Python spine owns the truth; agents are stateless workers at the edges.

```
                    ┌───────────────────────── SPINE (deterministic Python) ─────────────────────────┐
                    │                                                                                  │
  data sources ───► │   Supervisor ──► Job Queue ──► RPC Transport ──► [ Agent Runtime Pool ]          │
  (NIC lists,       │      ▲                                                   │                        │
   web, decks)      │      │            Postgres + pgvector  ◄── read/write ──┘  (the "blackboard")    │
                    │      └──────────── stage DAG advances on artifact writes ──────────────           │
                    └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
        Agents (isolated runtimes, star RPC to supervisor):
        • feynman  → crawl / enrich / extract        (spawn-per-job, web tools, bounded lifetime)
        • pi       → match-explain / draft / schedule (long-lived RPC worker pool, skills loaded)
```

- **Blackboard = the DB.** Agents read their inputs and write JSON artifacts back; the supervisor advances a stage DAG (ingest → enrich → extract → embed → match → explain/draft → approve → send). Agents never talk to each other — they coordinate through shared state. This is your "hybrid star" in practice.
- **Two lifecycles:** feynman crawlers are **spawned per job** (clean isolation, bounded cost, web tools); pi workers are a **long-lived RPC pool** (fast, skills preloaded, cheap per task).
- Deterministic code owns embeddings/vector index, dedup, scheduling math, and the human-approval gate.
- **Best fit** for all four of your answers.

## Approach B — Pure star RPC service mesh
Every agent is a long-lived RPC service; supervisor is a typed router; no DB-as-blackboard — results flow purely through RPC and the supervisor persists them. More "microservices" purity, but more failure-handling and reconnection logic to hand-build, and harder to demo/debug. Overkill for now.

## Approach C — One-shot CLI DAG
No long-lived processes; each stage is a `feynman <workflow>` or `pi -p --skill …` call orchestrated like a Makefile. Simplest and most reproducible, but throws away the "long-lived isolated runtime + RPC" property you specifically want, and re-pays startup cost every job.

**My recommendation: A.** It's the only one that satisfies isolated-runtime + RPC + agents-at-edges + demo-first simultaneously, and the DB-as-blackboard keeps the whole thing debuggable (you can inspect every artifact between stages).

Want me to build the detailed design on **Approach A**, or do you want to pull in an element of B (long-lived feynman crawlers too) or C (some stages as one-shot)?

content.text:
A. Here's the detailed design, section by section — I'll check in after each so we can adjust before it hardens.

---

## Section 1 — Components & the Agent Runtime Contract

This is the heart of your idea, so I'll be precise about it.

**The system is four layers:**

| Layer | What it is | Agent or code? |
|---|---|---|
| **Spine** | Supervisor, job queue, stage DAG, RPC transport | Deterministic Python |
| **Store** | Postgres + pgvector (profiles, artifacts, matches, jobs, events) | Code |
| **Agent fleet** | Isolated feynman/pi runtimes running your 3 skills | Agents |
| **Edges** | Data sources in; email/calendar out | External |

**An "agent" is a declarative spec the supervisor knows how to launch:**

```python
AgentSpec(
  name        = "enricher",
  runtime     = "feynman",          # feynman | pi
  skill       = "entity-enrichment", # discovered from .feynman/agent/skills/
  tools       = ["web_search","fetch_content","document_parse","write","read"],
  lifecycle   = "spawn_per_job",     # feynman crawlers = one-shot, isolated
  model       = None,                # None = current settings (deepseek-v4-pro)
  workdir     = "<job-scoped tmp>",  # isolation via --cwd / --session-dir
)
```

**Two lifecycles, matched to the runtime (this is a refinement of "RPC everywhere"):**

- **feynman crawlers → spawn-per-job, one-shot.** Launched as `feynman chat --new-session --cwd <jobdir> --prompt "<task>"`. The skill writes its JSON artifact into the job dir; the supervisor reads it back. Isolation comes free from separate process + `--cwd`. Crawlers *don't need persistent RPC* — a bounded process that produces an artifact and exits is cleaner and cheaper. (Your "can possibly communicate via RPC" was optional — I'm spending it where it pays.)

- **pi workers → long-lived RPC pool.** Launched once as `pi --mode rpc --session-id <w> --skill <path> --tools <…>`, kept warm. Supervisor sends `{"id","type":"prompt", <content>}` lines and reads streamed `{"type":"response",…}` back. This avoids re-paying model/skill load on every match or draft. *(One detail to pin during build: the exact content field name — my probe confirmed `type:"prompt"` is the discriminator; I'll nail `prompt`/`content` in the first implementation step.)*

**Why split this way:** crawling is bursty and I/O-bound (spawn, fetch, die); matching/drafting is chatty and latency-sensitive (keep warm). Same star topology — every agent connects only to the supervisor, never to each other.

**Isolation guarantees:** each agent gets its own OS process, its own `--session-dir`/`--cwd`, its own tool allowlist (a crawler literally cannot `write` outside its job dir if we don't grant it), and its own model budget. A crashed or runaway agent is killed and its job requeued without touching the spine or siblings.

Does this contract match what you had in mind — especially the two-lifecycle split (one-shot feynman crawlers, warm pi RPC pool)? Or did you want feynman crawlers held open on RPC too?

```
→  {"id": N, "type": "prompt", "message": "<task text>"}
←  … streamed event lines (assistant deltas, extension_ui_request, tool calls) …
←  {"id": N, "type": "response", "command": "prompt", "success": true}
```
Transport rule: collect event lines by `id` until the terminal `response` with matching `id`; ignore `extension_ui_request`/widget events.

**Updated contract:** both runtimes are **long-lived RPC pools** now — feynman crawlers held open too, exactly as you want. One consequence to design around: a warm feynman process accumulates session context across jobs, which can bleed between unrelated crawl targets. Mitigation: the supervisor issues a **per-job session reset** (a `new-session`/clear RPC command — I'll identify the exact one in build; worst case cycle the process every N jobs). So: `lifecycle = "pool"` for all agents, with session hygiene between jobs.

---

## Section 2 — The Spine (store + supervisor + stage DAG)

**Storage — Postgres + pgvector**, five tables:

| Table | Holds |
|---|---|
| `entities` | startups + partners (unified), profile JSON, `embedding vector(384)`, type, provenance |
| `artifacts` | every raw agent output (crawl dumps, extraction JSON) keyed by job — the audit trail / blackboard cells |
| `matches` | startup×partner, scores, rationale, draft email, status |
| `jobs` | queue rows: stage, agent, input ref, status, attempts, worker id |
| `events` | append-only log of every stage transition + agent call (observability) |

The DB **is** the blackboard: agents read an input artifact, write an output artifact, and the supervisor reacts to writes.

**Supervisor** (single Python process, async) does four things:
1. **Pool manager** — spawns/monitors the RPC pools (N feynman + M pi), health-checks, restarts on crash, enforces session hygiene.
2. **Scheduler** — pulls ready `jobs`, assigns to a free worker of the right agent type, writes the `message`, awaits the terminal response, persists the artifact.
3. **DAG engine** — declares stage dependencies; when a stage's artifact lands, it enqueues the next stage's jobs.
4. **Gatekeeper** — pauses the DAG at the human-approval stage (before any email/meeting side-effect).

**Stage DAG:**
```
ingest ─► enrich ─► extract ─► [embed+index] ─► match ─► explain+draft ─► ⏸ APPROVE ─► send+schedule
(code)   feynman   feynman      (code)          pi        pi              (human)      pi + MCP edge
```
Bracketed stages are deterministic code (embeddings, pgvector upsert, dedup, scheduling math). Everything else is an agent, and the approval gate is a hard stop.

Does this spine look right — particularly (a) Postgres+pgvector vs. a lighter SQLite+sqlite-vec for the demo, and (b) the DAG stage boundaries?

message.content: can we manage states of pools also to postgres? since the python code may not catch the whole live thing. debate me if need

content.text:
Good instinct, and mostly yes — but let me draw one line, because there's a distinction that matters.

**Two kinds of state are hiding in "the pool":**

1. **Control state** — which workers *should* exist, what job each holds, attempts, health, restart counts, session-reset counters. This is durable intent.
2. **Live channel** — the actual OS child process + its stdio pipe + the in-flight RPC stream buffer. This is a file descriptor bound to the supervisor's memory and the kernel.

**Your worry is right for (1) and I'll adopt it. For (2) it's physically impossible — and that's actually the key insight.**

Postgres can store a *record that a worker with pid X should be alive and is leasing job Y*. It cannot store the open pipe. If the supervisor dies, those pi/feynman children are orphaned and the pid in Postgres is instantly stale. So the answer isn't "put the live thing in Postgres" — it's **stop making Python's memory the source of truth at all.** Adopt the control-plane / data-plane split that Kubernetes and Nomad use:

- **Postgres = source of truth for desired + leased state** (durable, external, observable).
- **Supervisor = a reconciler** that on every tick makes reality match Postgres, and rebuilds its entire in-memory view from Postgres + an OS process scan after a crash.

This directly kills your concern: "Python may not catch the whole live thing" stops being a risk because Python is no longer the record — it's a disposable reconciler.

**Concretely, I add a `workers` table and three mechanics:**

```
workers: worker_id, agent_type, runtime, pid, boot_epoch,
         status(starting|idle|busy|draining|dead),
         current_job_id, started_at, last_heartbeat_at,
         jobs_since_reset, restart_count
```

- **Leases with expiry** on `jobs` (`leased_by`, `lease_expires_at`): a crashed worker's job auto-expires and is reclaimed. This is the durability that survives any crash.
- **Heartbeats**: supervisor pings each worker over RPC and writes `last_heartbeat_at`. Missed heartbeat → mark `dead` → lease expires → job requeued → replacement spawned.
- **Reconcile on boot**: read non-dead workers, check `pid` liveness against `boot_epoch`; adopt survivors, kill orphans from a prior epoch, mark the rest dead and reclaim their leases.

**Where I'll push back:** don't stream *token-level/partial* output into Postgres — that's write amplification for no gain. Persist **milestones** (job leased, artifact written, worker died), not the live byte stream. Keep partial output in a per-job log file. And heartbeats stay coarse (every 3–5s), not per-token.

Net: **control plane in Postgres, live channels reconciled in memory.** Stronger than your original framing, and it's the battle-tested orchestrator pattern. Since you're putting control state in Postgres, that also settles the earlier fork — **Postgres + pgvector, not SQLite.**

Fair? If you buy the control-plane/data-plane split, I'll fold the `workers` table + reconciler into the spine and move to Section 3 (end-to-end data flow + the human-approval gate + the external email/calendar boundary).

aiTitle: Review Feynman crawlers and RPC implementation

content.text:
Yes — and this is worth being precise about, because Postgres-as-the-log isn't just *easier* than a message queue here, it's **more correct**. Let me make the case and then draw the boundaries.

## Why Postgres beats an MQ for *this* saga

The classic failure of MQ-based sagas is the **dual-write problem**: you update your DB, then publish an event to Kafka — and if the publish fails, your state and your event stream disagree. People paper over it with the transactional-outbox pattern. With Postgres as the log you **delete the problem entirely**: the state transition and the event emission are the *same transaction*.

```sql
BEGIN;
  UPDATE saga_instances SET current_step='match', status='running' WHERE saga_id=$1;
  INSERT INTO events (saga_id, seq, kind, payload) VALUES ($1, $2, 'ExtractCompleted', $3);
  INSERT INTO jobs   (saga_id, stage, status) VALUES ($1, 'match', 'ready');   -- next command
COMMIT;
```
Atomic. No outbox, no broker to run, one datastore to inspect. Competing consumers come from `SELECT … FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`; push wake-ups from `LISTEN/NOTIFY` so the supervisor isn't busy-polling.

## Shape: orchestration saga, event-sourced

We already have a central Supervisor (star topology), so this is an **orchestration** saga, not choreography — the Supervisor *is* the saga coordinator. That maps cleanly onto three tables (folding in what we already had):

| Table | Role in the saga |
|---|---|
| `events` | **append-only log** — immutable facts (`ExtractCompleted`, `MatchFailed`, `Approved`, `EmailSent`, `MeetingScheduled`, `CompensationDone`). The source of truth. |
| `saga_instances` | **projection** — current folded state per run (`current_step`, `status`), rebuildable by replaying `events`. Fast to query for the dashboard. |
| `jobs` | **commands** — the next steps the Supervisor dispatches to agents. |

## Where I debate you — compensation is NOT uniform

Most of our steps have **no external side effect**, so they don't get compensations — they get **forward retry**:

```
ingest→enrich→extract→embed→match→draft   :  pure computation → retry forward, no compensation
⏸ approve                                  :  durable PARK state (see below)
send email                                 :  compensation = send retraction / flag human  (you can't "unsend")
schedule meeting                           :  compensation = Calendar API delete            (genuinely reversible)
```

Building compensations for pure-compute steps is wasted effort — if enrichment fails, you just re-run it. **Only the last two steps get backward recovery**, and be honest that "compensate an email" means *send a correction*, not undo. This keeps the saga small and truthful.

## The non-negotiable this forces on us

Postgres-as-queue with `SKIP LOCKED` + lease expiry is **at-least-once**: a stalled worker's job gets reclaimed and can run twice. So every step must be **idempotent keyed by `(saga_id, step)`** — and `send_email` in particular needs a unique guard (`UNIQUE(saga_id, 'send')`) so a double-dispatch physically cannot send twice. There is no "exactly once"; we get *at-least-once execution + idempotent effects = effectively once.* This idempotency work is the real cost of dropping the MQ, and it's cheap at our scale.

**Bonus you get for free:** the human-approval gate becomes a durable **park state** — the saga just sits at `awaiting_approval` in `saga_instances` until a human emits an `Approved`/`Rejected` event. An MQ would need a special parking mechanism; Postgres needs nothing.

**One honest ceiling:** Postgres-as-queue is excellent to dozens of workers / thousands of jobs/sec. NIC's world is dozens–hundreds of entities at a trickle, so you're nowhere near it — but I'm flagging it so it's a chosen tradeoff, not an accidental one.

If you're good with **orchestration saga + event-sourced Postgres log + compensation only on send/schedule + hard idempotency keys**, I'll fold this into the spine and present Section 3 (the end-to-end flow, now expressed as the saga: match → draft → park-for-approval → send → schedule, with the two compensations).

content.text:
Folded in. Here's the end-to-end flow, now expressed as sagas.

---

## Section 3 — Data Flow as Sagas

There are actually **two saga types**, and separating them keeps each honest about what it needs:

### Saga 1 — Onboarding (per entity) · *forward-recovery only, no compensations*

```
ingest ─► enrich ─► extract ─► embed+index ─► entity READY
(code)   feynman    feynman     (code)
seed     RPC pool   RPC pool    SBERT + pgvector upsert
```
Pure computation. Any step fails → retry forward (re-lease the job). Nothing to compensate. Terminal event: `EntityReady`. This unblocks matching.

### Saga 2 — Outreach (per startup) · *the real saga, with two compensations*

```
match ─► draft ─► ⏸ APPROVE ─► send ─► schedule ─► DONE
(pi)     (pi)     (human)      (code)   (code)
```

| Step | Actor | Success event | On failure | Compensation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `match` | pi (dealflow-match) | `MatchesRanked` | retry forward | — (no side effect) |
| `draft` | pi (dealflow-match §4) | `DraftReady` | retry forward | — |
| `approve` | **human** | `Approved` / `Rejected` | parks forever (durable) | `Rejected` → terminate saga |
| `send` | **code** → Gmail API | `EmailSent` | retry (idempotent guard) | send retraction / flag human |
| `schedule` | **code** → Calendar API | `MeetingScheduled` | retry | Calendar `delete` (truly reversible) |

**Why `send`/`schedule` are code, not agents:** the *draft* is fuzzy (agent), but the *send* is a mechanical, side-effectful API call that must be idempotent and guarded. That's exactly where deterministic code with a `UNIQUE(saga_id,'send')` key belongs — an agent doing it would reintroduce the double-send risk. This is "agents at the edges, code owns the irreversible I/O."

### The approval gate (durable park)

```
draft done ──► saga_instances.status = 'awaiting_approval'   (just sits in Postgres)
                        │
   NIC staff clicks Approve/Reject in UI
                        │
   INSERT INTO events (kind='Approved') ──► LISTEN/NOTIFY wakes supervisor ──► enqueue 'send' job
```
No timers, no MQ parking — the row waits. NIC staff see the draft + match rationale + proposed slots, edit if needed, then approve. Nothing irreversible happens before `Approved`.

### External boundary & demo safety

`send`/`schedule` run through an **adapter with a `dry_run` toggle**:
- `dry_run=true` (default for demo) → writes `EmailSent`/`MeetingScheduled` events with a rendered preview, **no real API call**. The whole saga runs end-to-end visibly and safely on stage.
- `dry_run=false` → real Gmail send (from NIC inbox, OAuth) + real Google Calendar event.

Same code path, same events, same idempotency — the toggle only swaps the adapter. So the demo shows a *complete* saga without risking a real email to a real VC, and flipping one flag makes it live.

### Concrete failure trace (what durability buys you)

```
send job leased by worker-3 → worker-3 stalls → lease expires (60s)
  → job reclaimed → BUT UNIQUE(saga_id,'send') already committed by worker-3's late finish
  → second attempt no-ops, folds EmailSent from the log → saga proceeds to schedule
  → schedule succeeds → MeetingScheduled → DONE
```
At-least-once dispatch, effectively-once effect. The event log is the arbiter.

---

That's the full spine + flow. Next is **Section 4 — tech stack, repo layout, agent-skill wiring, and the demo-first build sequence** (how the 3 existing skills slot into the pools, what's built in week 1 vs. later). Want me to go on, or adjust Section 3 first?

content.text:
Done — the human gate is out. The outreach saga is now fully automated end-to-end. Here's the revised Saga 2:

```
match ─► draft ─► verify ─► send ─► schedule ─► DONE
(pi)     (pi)     (pi)      (code)   (code)      no human anywhere
```

| Step | Actor | Success event | On failure |
|---|---|---|---|
| `match` | pi | `MatchesRanked` | retry forward |
| `draft` | pi | `DraftReady` | retry forward |
| `verify` | pi (LLM-as-judge) | `Verified` | reject → retry `draft` with feedback (max N), else dead-letter |
| `send` | code → Gmail | `EmailSent` | retry (idempotent guard) · compensation: retraction/flag |
| `schedule` | code → Calendar | `MeetingScheduled` | retry · compensation: Calendar delete |

**The `awaiting_approval` park state is deleted.** Nothing blocks; the saga runs to completion autonomously. Compensations on `send`/`schedule` stay (they're about failure recovery, not human sign-off).

**One thing I replaced the human with, and why — push back if you disagree.** I swapped the *human* gate for an **automated `verify` step**: a pi agent that checks the draft against the match facts before it sends — no hallucinated metrics, no invented meeting times, right language, on-brand for NIC. This is still 100% automated (an agent, not a person), and it directly enforces the anti-invention rules your own skills already declare ("Do NOT invent facts"). Without *some* checkpoint, a hallucinated claim goes out under NIC's name to a real VC with zero interception. `verify` is cheap insurance that keeps the "fully AI" property intact.

If you want it truly bare (`match → draft → send → schedule`, no verify at all), say so and I'll drop it. Otherwise `dry_run=true` still defaults on for the demo so nothing real sends until you flip it.

Want `verify` kept, and shall I move to **Section 4 (tech stack, repo layout, skill→pool wiring, demo-first build sequence)**?

content.text:
Removed. `send` and `schedule` are now **WIP / deferred** — the outreach saga ends by producing a stored, verified draft (plus suggested talking points) as *data*, not an action.

```
Outreach saga:  match ─► draft ─► verify ─► OutreachReady
                (pi)     (pi)     (pi)       stored in `matches`, status='draft_ready'

WIP (deferred):  send (Gmail API) · schedule (Calendar API)
```

**One honest consequence worth naming:** the compensations only existed for the side-effectful `send`/`schedule` steps. With those gone, **every remaining step is pure computation → the pipeline is now forward-recovery only, zero compensations.** So the saga machinery loses its "backward" half entirely.

That does *not* mean we drop the Postgres event-sourced design — it still earns its keep for:
- **crash recovery** (leases + reclaim on any worker/supervisor death),
- **at-least-once + idempotency** (`(saga_id, step)` keys),
- the **event log as audit trail** ("why did this match get drafted this way"),
- the **control-plane/data-plane worker management**.

We just keep the substrate with **no compensating actions defined** — the compensation path stays dormant until `send`/`schedule` land as WIP features, at which point it wakes up exactly where we designed it. So the architecture is forward-compatible, not over-built: I'll leave a clean seam (`compensations` registry, empty for now) rather than machinery you're not using.

**Net effect on the design:**
- External Gmail/Calendar boundary → **removed from scope** (becomes the WIP seam).
- `dry_run` toggle → moot for now (nothing sends).
- `matches` row terminal state = `draft_ready` holding scores + rationale + verified email draft (VI/EN).
- Meeting *slots* → the draft can *suggest* times as text, but real availability-check + booking is part of the deferred WIP.

Both sagas are now fully autonomous and side-effect-free — clean for the demo.

content.text:
## Section 4 — Stack, Layout, Skill Wiring & Build Sequence

### Tech stack

| Concern | Choice | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Language / deps | Python 3.12, **uv** | your standard; async-friendly |
| Supervisor | `asyncio` | one process juggling N RPC channels + Postgres LISTEN/NOTIFY |
| Store | **Postgres 16 + pgvector** | blackboard + event log + control plane in one |
| DB driver | `asyncpg` | native LISTEN/NOTIFY, `SKIP LOCKED` |
| Embeddings | `sentence-transformers` · `keepitreal/vietnamese-sbert` (384-d) | Vietnamese-first, matches research doc |
| Agent runtimes | **pi + feynman** via `--mode rpc` (subprocess + stdio JSON) | already installed, verified |
| Control/observability API | **FastAPI** | endpoints for jobs/events/sagas/workers |
| Demo dashboard | minimal (FastAPI + HTMX or a thin Next.js later) | watch the live pipeline + event log |
| Migrations | plain SQL files | no ORM ceremony at this scale |

### Repo layout

```
deal-flow-matchmaker/
├─ spine/
│  ├─ supervisor.py        # reconciler loop, scheduler, DAG engine, pool manager
│  ├─ transport.py         # spawn pi/feynman, stdio JSON-RPC, collect-by-id, ignore ui events
│  ├─ pools.py             # AgentSpec registry, lifecycle, session hygiene, heartbeats
│  ├─ sagas.py             # saga defs (onboarding, outreach), step→event map, compensations={} (seam)
│  ├─ store.py             # asyncpg access: jobs(SKIP LOCKED), events, saga_instances, workers, entities
│  └─ embed.py             # SBERT encode + pgvector upsert/search  (deterministic stages)
├─ agents/
│  └─ specs.yaml           # declarative: name, runtime, skill, tools, model, pool_size
├─ api/
│  └─ app.py               # FastAPI: /jobs /events /sagas /workers /entities /matches
├─ dashboard/              # minimal live view
├─ migrations/*.sql
├─ seed/                   # NIC 25 startups + curated partners (from research doc)
├─ .feynman/agent/skills/  # entity-enrichment · profile-extraction · dealflow-match  (already here)
└─ pyproject.toml
```

### How the 3 existing skills wire into the pools

The skills are the agents' **instruction sets for the fuzzy work**; their deterministic parts migrate to the spine. Explicit mapping:

| Pipeline step | Runtime + skill | Deterministic parts pulled into spine |
|---|---|---|
| `enrich` | **feynman** · `entity-enrichment` | — (web research is inherently agent) |
| `extract` | **feynman** · `profile-extraction` | — |
| `embed+index` | **code** (`embed.py`) | SBERT + pgvector (was `dealflow-match` Stage 2) |
| `match` (rank) | **code** (pgvector search) + rule filter | Stages 1–2 of `dealflow-match` become code |
| `draft` | **pi** · `dealflow-match` §3–4 | agent uses the skill's explanation + email prompts |
| `verify` | **pi** · LLM-as-judge (new tiny skill) | — |

So `dealflow-match` splits: its SBERT/rule logic becomes `embed.py`/match code; its LLM prompt sections drive the pi drafter. `entity-enrichment`/`profile-extraction` run whole on feynman. This is the "agents at edges, code owns the deterministic spine" principle applied concretely.

`agents/specs.yaml` example:
```yaml
- {name: enricher,  runtime: feynman, skill: entity-enrichment,   tools: [web_search,fetch_content,document_parse,write,read], pool_size: 2}
- {name: extractor, runtime: feynman, skill: profile-extraction,  tools: [document_parse,fetch_content,write,read],           pool_size: 1}
- {name: drafter,   runtime: pi,      skill: dealflow-match,       tools: [read,write],                                        pool_size: 2}
- {name: verifier,  runtime: pi,      skill: draft-verify,         tools: [read],                                              pool_size: 1}
```

### Demo-first build sequence (each phase ends verifiable)

| Phase | Deliverable | Verify by |
|---|---|---|
| **0 · Backbone** (riskiest first) | Postgres schema + migrations; `transport.py` RPC; one pi worker in a pool; supervisor loop with `SKIP LOCKED` + leases | dispatch a trivial prompt over RPC → response persisted as an event |
| **1 · Onboarding saga** | seed NIC entities → feynman `enrich`→`extract`→ code `embed+index` → `EntityReady` | 3 real NIC startups land as profiles + 384-d vectors in DB |
| **2 · Outreach saga** | code vector match → pi `draft` → pi `verify` → `draft_ready` | pick a startup → ranked partners + verified VI/EN draft |
| **3 · Observability + demo UI** | FastAPI + live dashboard (jobs/events/sagas/workers, event-log stream) | watch a full run advance stage-by-stage on screen |
| **4 · Hardening** | kill-a-worker crash test, idempotency test, session hygiene, dead-letter | job auto-reclaims after a mid-flight kill; no double-effect |
| **WIP seam** | `send`/`schedule` adapters (Gmail/Calendar) + wake dormant compensations | deferred |

**Seed data:** the research doc already lists 25 NIC-supported startups; combine with a curated partner set (investors/corporations/universities) so the enricher agents have real targets to crawl — that's your live "feynman crawlers" showcase.

**Two risks I'm flagging, not hiding:** (1) running several `deepseek-v4-pro` agents concurrently hits rate/cost limits — hence small `pool_size` + a global concurrency cap in the supervisor; (2) warm feynman sessions bleed context across crawl targets — hence the per-job session reset from Section 2.

---

That completes the design (Sections 1–4). If it looks right, I'll write it up as the spec doc at `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-18-agent-data-platform-design.md`, commit it, and then hand off to writing the implementation plan. Anything to adjust first?

content.text:
Good addition. Here's the honest "if needed" cut — containerize what genuinely benefits, don't force the agents in where it hurts.

## Section 4b — Docker Compose (infra only)

**Containerize the data plane; keep the agent runtimes on the host for now.** Reason: pi/feynman carry host-bound auth/config (`~/.pi/auth.json`, `feynman model login`, `pi-web-access`). Baking those into an image means mounting OAuth tokens and re-solving model login inside the container — real friction for no Phase-0 gain. The supervisor spawns pi/feynman as subprocesses, so it stays on the host beside them and connects to a **containerized Postgres** over `localhost:5432`.

```yaml
# docker-compose.yml
services:
  postgres:
    image: pgvector/pgvector:pg16          # pgvector preinstalled
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: dealflow
      POSTGRES_USER: dealflow
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: [REDACTED]
    ports: ["5432:5432"]
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data
      - ./migrations:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d:ro   # schema auto-applied on first boot
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U dealflow"]
      interval: 5s
      retries: 10

  api:                                     # optional; can also run on host during dev
    build: ./api
    depends_on:
      postgres: {condition: service_healthy}
    environment:
      DATABASE_URL: postgres://dealflow:[REDACTED:db-password]@postgres:5432/dealflow
    ports: ["8000:8000"]

volumes:
  pgdata:
```

**What runs where:**

| Component | Location | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Postgres + pgvector | **container** | reproducible, `docker compose up` = ready DB, migrations auto-applied |
| `api` + `dashboard` | container *or* host | stateless; container for a clean demo, host for fast dev |
| **supervisor + agent pools** | **host** | must spawn pi/feynman, which use host auth/config |

**Forward path (WIP seam), not now:** a `runtime` image that bakes pi+feynman and mounts `~/.pi` + `~/.feynman` read-only, moving the supervisor+pools fully into compose. I'll leave the Dockerfile stub but not wire it — same principle as the send/schedule seam: forward-compatible, not over-built.

**Build-sequence tweak:** Phase 0 now starts with `docker compose up postgres` before the backbone code, so migrations and pgvector exist from the first commit.

---

That rounds out the design. If you're good, I'll write the full spec to `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-18-agent-data-platform-design.md`, commit it, and then move to the implementation plan.

content.text:
Real gap — you're right. The design proved each *piece* is feasible but never defined how we prove the *whole thing* works, and with the human gate gone, data-quality verification is now the only thing standing between "AI onboarded real data" and "AI hallucinated a plausible VC." Let me close that with a verification section and a concrete end-state, anchored on your North Star.

## Section 5 — Verification, Testing & E2E Acceptance

### Your North Star, made measurable (the "definition of done")

| # | Acceptance criterion | How it's checked |
|---|---|---|
| A1 | **≥10 real NIC startups onboarded** (from the research-doc list) — enriched profile + 384-d embedding in Postgres, every field carrying a `source_url` | asserted: row counts, non-null embeddings; audited: provenance present |
| A2 | **≥10 real Vietnam-based investors** onboarded as partners with provenance | asserted + audited |
| A3 | **Every startup has ranked matches** — top-K partners with composite score + LLM rationale + verified VI/EN draft | asserted: ≥1 match/startup, non-null rationale/draft |
| A4 | **Simple dashboard** lists entities; clicking a startup shows its ranked matches + rationale + draft | eyeball on screen |
| A5 | **Reproducible from zero**: `docker compose up postgres` → supervisor → seed/trigger → all of the above | one scripted run |

I'll treat these as the target (numbers adjustable). This is what the final demo shows.

### Testing strategy — layered, because agents aren't deterministic

You can't assert exact LLM output, so we test **structure and invariants** for agent steps and **exact behavior** for the spine:

| Layer | Scope | What's asserted | Determinism |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unit** | `store`, `embed`, `sagas`, `transport` | lease acquire/expiry/reclaim; idempotency-key uniqueness; SBERT dims=384; pgvector ranking order; event-fold → projection; RPC collect-by-id + ignore `extension_ui_request` | fully deterministic — TDD these |
| **RPC integration** | real pi + feynman, cheap prompts | worker answers a trivial prompt; feynman has `web_search`; **kill-mid-job → job reclaimed + requeued** | deterministic control flow |
| **Agent contract** | enricher/extractor/drafter/verifier outputs | JSON matches schema; **invariants**: angel profiles carry no PII; drafts invent no meeting times/metrics (this is what `verify` enforces); enriched fields have `source_url` | schema + invariants, not exact text |
| **E2E acceptance** | full pipeline on real seed | A1–A5 above | counts asserted + human eyeball |

Per your team standards I'll **present the test plan before code and TDD the deterministic spine**; agent steps get schema/invariant tests.

### Reviews — two gates, one of which replaces the human you removed

1. **Code review** after each phase (`requesting-code-review`) — spine correctness (leases, idempotency, reconciliation) is where bugs bite.
2. **Data-quality / provenance audit** on the onboarded data — *this is the safeguard that replaces the human approval gate.* Because `source_url` is mandatory in the skills' output schema, we can mechanically flag any field with no source as suspect-hallucination, and spot-check a sample of real profiles against their cited URLs. Fully-automated onboarding is only trustworthy if we can prove the data is sourced, not invented.

### One honesty note on "find investors in Vietnam"

The enricher enriches a *named* entity from the web; open-ended "discover every VN investor from scratch" is a weaker, flakier crawl. For a reliable demo I'll **seed ~15–20 real known Vietnam funds/angels by name** (Mekong Capital, Do Ventures, VinVentures, 500 Global VN, Nextrans, ThinkZone, Genesia, Touchstone, etc.) and let feynman enrich each — real data, high reliability. A best-effort feynman **discovery agent** (web-search for more names) can append to that as a bonus, not the backbone.

### Revised build sequence (tests + acceptance interleaved)

| Phase | Adds | Exit gate |
|---|---|---|
| **0 · Backbone** | compose Postgres, transport, pool, supervisor + **unit tests** (lease/idempotency/RPC framing) | trivial RPC round-trip persisted; crash-reclaim test green |
| **1 · Onboarding saga** | seed → feynman enrich/extract → code embed+index + **contract tests** | 3 real NIC startups onboarded with provenance + vectors |
| **2 · Outreach saga** | vector match → pi draft → pi verify + **invariant tests** | startup → ranked partners + verified draft |
| **3 · Dashboard + API** | FastAPI + simple live view | watch a full run advance on screen |
| **4 · Hardening** | crash/idempotency/session-hygiene suite | kill worker mid-job → no double-effect, auto-recovery |
| **5 · E2E ACCEPTANCE** | scale seed to full NIC set + VN investors; **run A1–A5**; provenance audit; code review | **all of A1–A5 pass from a cold `docker compose up`** — this is the deliverable |

---

Phase 5 is now the explicit "prove it works end-to-end with real data" gate you asked for. Good to fold all of this (Sections 1–5) into the spec doc and commit, then move to the implementation plan? Or adjust the acceptance targets first?

Confirmed — that matches the seam I proposed. The one thing I'll make explicit so "later" is a config swap rather than a rewrite: a **Launcher abstraction** in `transport.py`.

```python
class RuntimeLauncher(Protocol):
    async def spawn(self, spec: AgentSpec) -> RpcChannel: ...

# now:   LocalProcessLauncher  → subprocess pi/feynman on host (uses host ~/.pi, ~/.feynman auth)
# later: ContainerLauncher     → same RPC channel, process lives in a container
```

The supervisor only knows `RpcChannel` — it never cares *where* the runtime runs. So the "support pi/feynman builds later" requirement becomes: add `runtime/Dockerfile` (bakes pi+feynman, mounts auth read-only), add an `agent-runtime` service to compose under a `profiles: [full]` guard, and select `ContainerLauncher` via `agents/specs.yaml`. Present as a stub now, wired later.

**Compose scope now:** `postgres` + `api` only (as you said). Supervisor + pools run on host via `LocalProcessLauncher`.

That settles the whole design. Writing it up now.

input.content:
# Agent Data Platform for Deal-flow Matchmaking — Design Spec

- **Date:** 2026-07-18
- **Problem:** #135 Deal-flow Matchmaker (VAIC 2026, sponsor NIC)
- **Status:** Design approved (Sections 1–5), pending spec review → implementation plan

---

## 1. Summary

Build the deal-flow matchmaker (problem #135) as a **data platform whose workers are AI agent runtimes**. The compute at the fuzzy edges — crawling, enrichment, extraction, match explanation, draft generation, verification — runs inside **isolated `feynman` and `pi` processes** driven over a **stdio JSON-RPC** protocol. A **deterministic Python spine** (supervisor + Postgres) owns storage, orchestration, the vector index, and all irreversible I/O.

The pipeline is modeled as **orchestration sagas** whose event log lives in Postgres (no message queue). The system is **fully automated — no human-in-the-loop** — so a **data-quality/provenance audit** is the safeguard against hallucinated data.

### North Star (definition of done)

A cold `docker compose up postgres` → start supervisor → seed/trigger produces:

- **A1** ≥10 real NIC startups onboarded (enriched profile + 384-d embedding, every field carrying a `source_url`).
- **A2** ≥10 real Vietnam-based investors onboarded as partners, with provenance.
- **A3** Every startup has ranked matches: top-K partners + composite score + LLM rationale + verified VI/EN draft.
- **A4** A simple dashboard lists entities; clicking a startup shows its ranked matches + rationale + draft.
- **A5** The whole thing is reproducible from zero via one scripted run.

Numbers are targets, adjustable.

---

## 2. Decisions (locked)

| # | Decision | Choice |
|---|---|---|
| D1 | Scope | Serious foundation, demo-first |
| D2 | Agent boundary | **Agents at the edges, deterministic code at the spine** |
| D3 | RPC topology | **Star** — supervisor ↔ each agent; agents never talk directly |
| D4 | Runtime split | **feynman** for crawlers (web tools), **pi** for match/draft/verify |
| D5 | Agent lifecycle | **Long-lived RPC pools** for both runtimes, with per-job session hygiene |
| D6 | Coordination | **Orchestration saga**, event-sourced in **Postgres** (no MQ) |
| D7 | Human approval | **Removed** — fully automated; provenance audit is the safeguard |
| D8 | Side effects | `send`/`schedule` **deferred to WIP**; pipeline currently side-effect-free |
| D9 | Infra | **Docker Compose for `postgres` + `api`**; supervisor+pools on host; pi/feynman containerization supported later via a Launcher seam |

---

## 3. Architecture

Four layers:

| Layer | Contents | Agent or code |
|---|---|---|
| **Spine** | Supervisor (reconciler, scheduler, DAG engine, pool manager), RPC transport | deterministic Python |
| **Store** | Postgres + pgvector (entities, artifacts, matches, jobs, events, saga_instances, workers) | code |
| **Agent fleet** | isolated feynman/pi runtimes running the 3 domain skills + a verify skill | agents |
| **Edges** | data sources in; email/calendar out (WIP) | external |

```
                    ┌──────────────── SPINE (deterministic Python) ────────────────┐
 data sources ───►  │  Supervisor ──► jobs (SKIP LOCKED) ──► RPC transport ──► pools │
 (NIC lists, web)   │      ▲                                          │              │
                    │      │   Postgres + pgvector  ◄── read/write ───┘  (blackboard)│
                    │      └──── DAG advances on artifact/event writes ──────────────│
                    └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
   Agent pools (star RPC to supervisor):
   • feynman  → enrich / extract           (web tools)
   • pi       → draft / verify             (skills, cheap)
   Deterministic spine steps: ingest, embed+index, match(rank), (send/schedule = WIP)
```

The DB **is** the blackboard: agents read an input artifact and write an output artifact; the supervisor reacts to writes and advances the stage DAG. Agents coordinate only through shared state.

---

## 4. Agent Runtime Contract

An agent is a declarative spec the supervisor knows how to launch:

```python
AgentSpec(
  name="enricher", runtime="feynman", skill="entity-enrichment",
  tools=["web_search","fetch_content","document_parse","write","read"],
  lifecycle="pool", pool_size=2, model=None,  # None = current settings (deepseek-v4-pro)
)
```

### RPC protocol (verified on both `pi` and `feynman`)

Launch: `pi --mode rpc --session-id <w> --skill <path> --tools <…>` / `feynman --mode rpc --new-session`.

```
→  {"id": N, "type": "prompt", "message": "<task text>"}
←  … streamed event lines (assistant deltas, tool calls, extension_ui_request/widget) …
←  {"id": N, "type": "response", "command": "prompt", "success": true}
```

**Transport rules:**
- The request discriminator is `type`; the content field is **`message`** (verified — `text`/`prompt`/`content` fail).
- Collect event lines by matching `id` until the terminal `{"type":"response", id:N}`.
- **Ignore** `extension_ui_request` / `setWidget` event lines (feynman emits them).

### Isolation & lifecycle

- Each agent = its own OS process, own `--session-dir`/`--cwd`, own tool allowlist, own model budget.
- **Both runtimes are long-lived RPC pools.** Warm feynman sessions accumulate context, so the supervisor performs a **per-job session reset** (a new-session/clear RPC command, identified in build; worst case cycle the process every N jobs). Tracked via `workers.jobs_since_reset`.
- A crashed/runaway agent is killed and its job requeued without touching the spine or siblings.

### Launcher seam (host now, container later)

```python
class RuntimeLauncher(Protocol):
    async def spawn(self, spec: AgentSpec) -> RpcChannel: ...
# now:   LocalProcessLauncher (host subprocess, host ~/.pi & ~/.feynman auth)
# later: ContainerLauncher    (same RpcChannel, runtime in a container)
```
The supervisor only sees `RpcChannel`; where the runtime runs is a config swap.

---

## 5. The Spine

### 5.1 Storage schema (Postgres + pgvector)

```sql
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS vector;

-- unified startups + partners
CREATE TABLE entities (
  id            TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  type          TEXT NOT NULL,          -- startup | investor | corporation | university | research_institution
  name          TEXT NOT NULL,
  profile       JSONB NOT NULL,          -- provenance-tracked fields ({value, source_url, confidence})
  embedding     vector(384),
  status        TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'seeded',  -- seeded | enriching | ready
  created_at    TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now(),
  updated_at    TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now()
);
CREATE INDEX ON entities USING ivfflat (embedding vector_cosine_ops);

-- raw agent outputs (blackboard cells / audit trail)
CREATE TABLE artifacts (
  id          BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  saga_id     TEXT NOT NULL,
  step        TEXT NOT NULL,
  entity_id   TEXT,
  payload     JSONB NOT NULL,
  created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now()
);

-- domain results
CREATE TABLE matches (
  id             BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  startup_id     TEXT REFERENCES entities(id),
  partner_id     TEXT REFERENCES entities(id),
  composite      REAL, semantic REAL, sector_overlap REAL,
  rationale_en   TEXT, rationale_vi TEXT,
  draft_en       TEXT, draft_vi TEXT,
  status         TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'ranked',  -- ranked | draft_ready
  created_at     TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now(),
  UNIQUE (startup_id, partner_id)
);

-- commands (the queue)
CREATE TABLE jobs (
  id               BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  saga_id          TEXT NOT NULL,
  stage            TEXT NOT NULL,          -- enrich | extract | embed | match | draft | verify
  agent            TEXT,                   -- agent name (null for code stages)
  input_ref        JSONB,
  status           TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'ready', -- ready | leased | done | failed | dead
  attempts         INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  leased_by        TEXT,
  lease_expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
  UNIQUE (saga_id, stage)                  -- idempotency key
);
CREATE INDEX ON jobs (status, stage);

-- append-only event log (source of truth)
CREATE TABLE events (
  id          BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  saga_id     TEXT NOT NULL,
  seq         INT NOT NULL,
  kind        TEXT NOT NULL,               -- EntityReady | MatchesRanked | DraftReady | Verified | ...
  payload     JSONB,
  created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now(),
  UNIQUE (saga_id, seq)
);

-- folded projection (fast queries; rebuildable from events)
CREATE TABLE saga_instances (
  saga_id       TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  type          TEXT NOT NULL,             -- onboarding | outreach
  subject_id    TEXT,                      -- entity/startup id
  current_step  TEXT,
  status        TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'running',  -- running | done | failed
  updated_at    TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now()
);

-- control plane: worker registry
CREATE TABLE workers (
  worker_id         TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  agent_type        TEXT NOT NULL,
  runtime           TEXT NOT NULL,         -- pi | feynman
  pid               INT,
  boot_epoch        TEXT NOT NULL,         -- supervisor boot id (orphan detection)
  status            TEXT NOT NULL,         -- starting | idle | busy | draining | dead
  current_job_id    BIGINT,
  jobs_since_reset  INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  restart_count     INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  started_at        TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now(),
  last_heartbeat_at TIMESTAMPTZ
);
```

### 5.2 Supervisor (single async process)

1. **Pool manager** — spawns/monitors RPC pools via the Launcher, health-checks, restarts on crash, enforces session hygiene.
2. **Scheduler** — `SELECT … FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` to lease ready jobs; assigns to a free worker of the right type; sends the `message`; awaits terminal response; persists artifact + event.
3. **DAG engine** — when a stage's event lands, enqueues the next stage's jobs (via `LISTEN/NOTIFY`).
4. **Compensation registry** — empty now (no side effects); wakes when send/schedule land.

### 5.3 Control plane / data plane (crash recovery)

Postgres is the source of truth for **desired + leased** state; the supervisor is a **reconciler**, not the record.

- **Leases with expiry**: a crashed worker's job auto-expires and is reclaimed.
- **Heartbeats** (coarse, every 3–5s): missed → mark `dead` → lease expires → job requeued → replacement spawned.
- **Reconcile on boot**: read non-dead workers; check `pid` liveness vs `boot_epoch`; adopt survivors, kill prior-epoch orphans, mark the rest dead and reclaim leases.
- Persist **milestones** only (job leased, artifact written, worker died) — never token-level streams.

### 5.4 Saga model (Postgres-as-log, no MQ)

State transition + event emission are one transaction — eliminates the dual-write problem:

```sql
BEGIN;
  UPDATE saga_instances SET current_step='match', status='running' WHERE saga_id=$1;
  INSERT INTO events (saga_id, seq, kind, payload) VALUES ($1,$2,'ExtractCompleted',$3);
  INSERT INTO jobs   (saga_id, stage, status)      VALUES ($1,'match','ready');
COMMIT;
```

- **At-least-once + idempotency**: `UNIQUE(saga_id, stage)` guards double-dispatch. There is no exactly-once; effectively-once = at-least-once execution + idempotent effects.
- Competing consumers via `SKIP LOCKED`; push wake-ups via `LISTEN/NOTIFY`.

---

## 6. Data Flow (two sagas)

### Saga 1 — Onboarding (per entity) · forward-recovery only

```
ingest ─► enrich ─► extract ─► embed+index ─► EntityReady
(code)   feynman    feynman     (code: SBERT + pgvector)
```
Pure computation. Any step fails → retry forward. No compensations.

### Saga 2 — Outreach (per startup) · forward-recovery only (currently)

```
match ─► draft ─► verify ─► OutreachReady (matches.status='draft_ready')
(code)   (pi)     (pi: LLM-as-judge)
```

| Step | Actor | Success event | On failure |
|---|---|---|---|
| match (rank) | code (rule filter + pgvector search) | `MatchesRanked` | retry forward |
| draft | pi · dealflow-match §3–4 prompts | `DraftReady` | retry forward |
| verify | pi · draft-verify (checks facts/PII/language, no invented times) | `Verified` | reject → retry draft w/ feedback (max N) → dead-letter |

**WIP seam (deferred):** `send` (Gmail API, `UNIQUE(saga_id,'send')` guard, compensation = retraction) and `schedule` (Calendar API, compensation = event delete). The compensation registry and `dry_run` adapter toggle are designed in but not wired.

---

## 7. Skill Wiring

The 3 existing skills in `.feynman/agent/skills/` are the agents' instruction sets for fuzzy work; their deterministic parts migrate to the spine.

| Step | Runtime + skill | Deterministic parts → spine |
|---|---|---|
| enrich | feynman · `entity-enrichment` | — |
| extract | feynman · `profile-extraction` | — |
| embed+index | **code** (`embed.py`) | SBERT + pgvector (was dealflow-match Stage 2) |
| match (rank) | **code** | dealflow-match Stages 1–2 |
| draft | pi · `dealflow-match` §3–4 | agent uses the skill's explanation + email prompts |
| verify | pi · `draft-verify` (new tiny skill) | — |

`entity-enrichment` mandates `source_url` per field — this is what makes the provenance audit (§9) possible.

---

## 8. Tech Stack & Repo Layout

| Concern | Choice |
|---|---|
| Language / deps | Python 3.12, **uv** |
| Supervisor | `asyncio` |
| Store | Postgres 16 + pgvector (`pgvector/pgvector:pg16`) |
| DB driver | `asyncpg` (LISTEN/NOTIFY, SKIP LOCKED) |
| Embeddings | `sentence-transformers` · `keepitreal/vietnamese-sbert` (384-d) |
| Agent runtimes | pi + feynman via `--mode rpc` |
| API / dashboard | FastAPI (+ HTMX or thin Next.js) |
| Migrations | plain SQL |

```
deal-flow-matchmaker/
├─ spine/{supervisor,transport,pools,sagas,store,embed}.py
├─ agents/specs.yaml            # declarative agent registry
├─ api/app.py                   # FastAPI: /jobs /events /sagas /workers /entities /matches
├─ dashboard/                   # minimal live view
├─ migrations/*.sql
├─ seed/                        # NIC 25 startups + curated VN investors
├─ runtime/Dockerfile           # STUB: pi+feynman baked (wired later)
├─ docker-compose.yml           # postgres + api
├─ .feynman/agent/skills/       # entity-enrichment · profile-extraction · dealflow-match (+ draft-verify)
└─ pyproject.toml
```

### docker-compose.yml (now: postgres + api)

```yaml
services:
  postgres:
    image: pgvector/pgvector:pg16
    environment: {POSTGRES_DB: dealflow, POSTGRES_USER: dealflow, POSTGRES_PASSWORD: [REDACTED]}
    ports: ["5432:5432"]
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data
      - ./migrations:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d:ro
    healthcheck: {test: ["CMD-SHELL","pg_isready -U dealflow"], interval: 5s, retries: 10}
  api:
    build: ./api
    depends_on: {postgres: {condition: service_healthy}}
    environment: {DATABASE_URL: "postgres://dealflow:[REDACTED:db-password]@postgres:5432/dealflow"}
    ports: ["8000:8000"]
  # agent-runtime:   # profiles: [full] — WIP: bakes pi+feynman, mounts ~/.pi & ~/.feynman
volumes: {pgdata: {}}
```

Supervisor + agent pools run on **host** via `LocalProcessLauncher`, connecting to Postgres at `localhost:5432`.

---

## 9. Verification, Testing & Reviews

### Testing layers

| Layer | Scope | Asserted | Determinism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unit | store, embed, sagas, transport | lease acquire/expiry/reclaim; idempotency uniqueness; SBERT dim=384; pgvector ranking; event-fold→projection; RPC collect-by-id + ignore ui events | fully deterministic — **TDD** |
| RPC integration | real pi + feynman, cheap prompts | worker answers trivial prompt; feynman has `web_search`; **kill-mid-job → reclaimed + requeued** | deterministic control flow |
| Agent contract | enricher/extractor/drafter/verifier | JSON matches schema; **invariants**: no PII in angel profiles, no invented times/metrics, `source_url` present | schema + invariants |
| E2E acceptance | full pipeline on real seed | A1–A5 | counts asserted + eyeball |

Per team standards: **present test plan before code; TDD the deterministic spine.**

### Reviews (two gates)

1. **Code review** each phase — spine correctness (leases, idempotency, reconciliation).
2. **Data-quality / provenance audit** — *replaces the removed human gate.* Mechanically flag any onboarded field lacking `source_url` as suspect-hallucination; spot-check a sample against cited URLs. Fully-automated onboarding is trustworthy only if provably sourced.

### "Find investors in Vietnam" approach

Seed ~15–20 **real known VN funds/angels by name** (Mekong Capital, Do Ventures, VinVentures, 500 Global VN, Nextrans, ThinkZone, Genesia, Touchstone, …) → feynman enriches each (reliable). An optional feynman **discovery agent** (web-search for more names) appends as a bonus, not the backbone.

---

## 10. Build Sequence (tests + acceptance interleaved)

| Phase | Adds | Exit gate |
|---|---|---|
| **0 · Backbone** | compose Postgres, transport, pool, supervisor + unit tests (lease/idempotency/RPC framing) | trivial RPC round-trip persisted; crash-reclaim test green |
| **1 · Onboarding saga** | seed → feynman enrich/extract → code embed+index + contract tests | 3 real NIC startups onboarded w/ provenance + vectors |
| **2 · Outreach saga** | vector match → pi draft → pi verify + invariant tests | startup → ranked partners + verified draft |
| **3 · Dashboard + API** | FastAPI + simple live view | watch a full run advance on screen |
| **4 · Hardening** | crash/idempotency/session-hygiene suite | kill worker mid-job → no double-effect, auto-recovery |
| **5 · E2E ACCEPTANCE** | scale seed to full NIC set + VN investors; run A1–A5; provenance audit; code review | **all of A1–A5 pass from a cold `docker compose up`** — the deliverable |

---

## 11. Risks

| Risk | Mitigation |
|---|---|
| Concurrent `deepseek-v4-pro` agents hit rate/cost limits | small `pool_size` + global concurrency cap in supervisor |
| Warm feynman session context bleed across crawl targets | per-job session reset; cycle process every N jobs |
| Fully-automated data may hallucinate | mandatory `source_url` + provenance audit gate |
| Orphaned agent processes after supervisor crash | `boot_epoch` tagging + kill prior-epoch orphans on reconcile |
| pi/feynman auth coupling blocks containerization | Launcher seam; host now, `ContainerLauncher` + mounted auth later |

---

## 12. WIP Seams (designed, not built)

- `send` (Gmail) + `schedule` (Calendar) steps, their compensations, and the `dry_run` adapter toggle.
- `ContainerLauncher` + `runtime/Dockerfile` + compose `agent-runtime` service (`profiles: [full]`).
- feynman discovery agent for open-ended investor discovery.
